title: Why Multiple Angles Help
concept: Each rephrasing surfaces slightly different documents, increasing overall recall versus a single query.

exercises.prompt: What is the basic workflow of Multi-Query Retrieval that we learned in the last lesson?

options.text: It translates the query into multiple foreign languages.
It writes multiple versions of a query, searches for each, and merges the unique results.
It splits one long query into single words and searches for each word separately.

exercises.explain: Multi-query retrieval generates several phrasings of a user's question, searches the database for each one, and then merges the results while removing duplicates.

cards.title: Lost in Translation
cards.body: Have you ever asked a search engine a question, got zero good results, and then reworded it to instantly find what you needed? Databases struggle with the same language limitations.

cards.title: The Power of Recall
cards.body: Multi-query retrieval works because different phrasings of the same question sit in different spots in vector space. Running all of them retrieves a wider, more complete set of relevant documents.

cards.title: The House Windows
cards.body: Think of searching for a missing cat. If you only look from the front porch, you miss the backyard. Checking from three different windows gives you a complete view of the yard.

cards.title: Rephrasing in Action
cards.body: If a user asks 'How do I fix a leaky pipe?', a single search might miss documents titled 'Plumbing repair guide'. Rephrasing it to 'Steps for repairing water pipes' catches that missing guide.

cards.title: The Perfect Search Myth
cards.body: You might think vector search is smart enough to find everything with one query. In reality, vector embeddings are sensitive to specific wording, meaning single queries often leave huge blind spots.

cards.title: Narrow vs. Wide Net
cards.body: A single query only grabs documents in one tight cluster. Multiple queries cast a wider net, capturing diverse documents across the vector space.

left.text: Retrieves only direct matches close to the exact phrasing. Low recall.

right.text: Retrieves documents from multiple phrasing clusters. High recall.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of retrieving documents for multiple variations of a user query?

options.text: It speeds up the search database response time.
It retrieves different documents, raising the chances of finding the true answer.
It reduces the token usage of the LLM.

exercises.explain: Because different words project to slightly different areas in vector space, using multiple variations helps grab relevant documents that a single search query would overlook.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A single query can miss relevant documents that use different words, even when using vector search.
exercises.explain: Even advanced vector embeddings are sensitive to the specific words used, so varying the query helps locate documents that match the meaning but use different terminology.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the main benefit of searching with multiple query variations:
exercises.sentence: Using multiple search variations helps us maximize recall by finding documents that a single search would miss.
exercises.explain: Recall measures how many of the truly relevant documents we successfully fetched. Multi-query retrieval is designed specifically to boost this metric.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following looks like an AI-generated set of query variations designed to improve recall?

options.text: Query: 'How to learn python?' -> 'How to learn python? How to learn python?'
Query: 'How to learn python?' -> Variations: ['Python tutorials for beginners', 'Best way to self-teach Python', 'Guide to starting with Python programming']
Query: 'How to learn python?' -> 'SELECT * FROM courses WHERE language = "python"'

exercises.explain: Option B shows a typical LLM-generated output that systematically expands a single query into distinct linguistic variations to capture different documents.

exercises.prompt: Why is sending multiple rephrased queries to a vector database better than just sending one when building a search assistant?

options.text: It reduces the total number of documents we have to process.
It catches documents that use different terminology but answer the same core question.
It guarantees that the LLM will find the answer faster with fewer API calls.
It completely replaces the need for vector databases.

exercises.explain: By asking for the same concept in different ways (like asking for a tutorial, an example, or a guide), we retrieve diverse documents that a single query would miss, drastically boosting recall before feeding them to the LLM.
